data.description:
## Why
Three sources disagree on the lock lifecycle. `docs/concurrency.md:107-117` still documents
the bash `_flock_stage_commit` fallback as the active write contract; `src/rebar/_store/
lock.py:7-23` says the dual-lock arrangement is a migration window that flips
`dual_window -> False` "after the bash core is retired" (yet still defaults
`dual_window=True` at `:274`); and `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py:3-10,120`
states the bash write core has ALREADY been retired. A test cannot reliably enforce a
contract while the docs give incompatible lifecycle states. VALIDATED.

## What
Decide whether `dual_window=True` is a permanent compatibility contract or migration
residue, then make all three sources consistent (flip/remove the default and simplify to
plain fcntl if residue; otherwise document it as intentional and permanent).

## Scope
- `docs/concurrency.md`, `src/rebar/_store/lock.py`, `tests/integration/test_store_
  concurrency.py` (and any dual-lock call sites).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A single, consistent lifecycle statement across the doc, the lock module, and the
      integration test.
- [ ] If residue: `dual_window` default and the mkdir/flock dual path are removed or gated,
      and `concurrency.md` stops describing the retired bash path as active.
- [ ] If permanent: the "migration window" language is replaced with an intentional-contract
      note and a test enforces it.
- [ ] `make lint && make test` green.
data.title: Settle the dual_window lock-portability lifecycle across docs, lock module, and tests
